task_updates.executor_notes: Rewired the imperative timeline sub-hooks to prefer the provider-scoped Zustand store boundary while preserving their existing control flow and load-bearing refs. `useClipDrag` and `useClipResizeGesture` now resolve store-backed data/ops/mutable adapters when the store provider is mounted, but keep their pointer-session refs and fallback paths for test harnesses. `useAssetManagement` and `useExternalDrop` now read latest data/selection/mutation callbacks from the store at callback time, which removes the old context-identity coupling without broad subscriptions in hot handlers. `useTimelinePersistence` and `usePollSync` now resolve `dataRef`, `pendingOpsRef`, and `interactionStateRef` through the store when available, while preserving the async/gesture refs that guard save and poll sequencing. Added `TimelineStoreProvider` around the existing legacy providers so this batch can move the high-risk hooks onto the store boundary without deleting the old contexts yet. Verified with `npx tsc --noEmit --pretty false` and full-file vitest runs for `useClipDrag`, `useExternalDrop`, `useTimelinePersistence`, and `VideoEditorProvider`.

sense_check_acknowledgments.executor_note: Yes. The drag, resize, external-drop, asset-management, persistence, and poll hooks still own their original imperative state machines; the migration only swapped how they source timeline state and ops. The load-bearing refs identified in the audit (`dataRef`, `pendingOpsRef`, `interactionStateRef`, `selectedClipIdsRef`, `additiveSelectionRef`) were preserved and are now resolved through the store boundary when mounted. Only the old unstable-context-object dependency path was reduced by reading latest slices/adapters from the store at callback time.
